14	# Blueprint Clean Subagent
15	
16	Your job is to read and refine a blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) to ensure it meets strict quality, purity, and citation standards.
17	
18	## Your Tasks

22	   - Remove project history references like "since iteration N", "our failed route", etc.
23	   - Ensure the blueprint reads as a timeless, standalone mathematical document.
24	2. **Trim Verbosity:**
25	   - Remove useless, overly conversational paragraphs.
26	   - Remove passages that are irrelevant or redundantly discuss things already implemented, unless they serve a mathematical purpose.
27	   - Keep the prose concise and mathematically precise.
28	3. **Enforce Citation Discipline & Add Quotes:**

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |

63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.

126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.

135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	\chapter{Section graded ring infrastructure: tensor powers and graded sections}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SectionGradedRing}
3	
4	\providecommand{\PshMod}{\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}}
5	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
6	\providecommand{\Modules}{\mathrm{Modules}}
7	
8	The Hilbert-polynomial route of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} represents the
9	degree-\(m\) graded component of a fibre by the global sections
10	\(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) of a twist of a coherent
11	sheaf by a tensor power of a line bundle, and it organises these components into
12	a graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
13	(\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and a graded module
14	\(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
15	\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
16	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}). Three pieces of that picture have no direct
17	Mathlib counterpart and must be built here, each in its own section:
18	
19	\begin{enumerate}
20	  \item \textbf{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
21	    (\cref{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}). Mathlib equips the category
22	    \(\PshMod\) of \emph{presheaves} of modules over a presheaf of commutative
23	    rings with a symmetric monoidal structure
24	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) and provides the
25	    sheafification functor \(\PshMod \to \ShMod\)
26	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); but the category
27	    \(\ShMod\) of \emph{sheaves} of modules carries \emph{no} monoidal
28	    structure. The tensor product of sheaves of modules
29	    \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) must therefore be built
30	    as the sheafification of the pointwise tensor presheaf, and the iterated
31	    tensor power \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) recursively from it.
32	  \item \textbf{Lax-monoidal global sections} (\cref{sec:sgr_lax_sections}).
33	    The global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is only \emph{lax} monoidal: from the
34	    pointwise-strict monoidality of the presheaf tensor product and the
35	    sheafification unit one obtains a natural multiplication
36	    \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_X)} \Gamma(\mathcal{G})
37	    \to \Gamma(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\), together with
38	    the associativity and unit coherence those products must satisfy.
39	  \item \textbf{Graded-ring / graded-module assembly}
40	    (\cref{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}). The section multiplications and the
41	    tensor-power comparison isomorphisms assemble, through Mathlib's
42	    external-direct-sum graded API (\cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib},
43	    \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}), into the graded \emph{ring} structure
44	    on \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and the graded
45	    \emph{module} structure on
46	    \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
47	\end{enumerate}
48	
49	\section{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
50	\label{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}
51	
52	We work over a fixed scheme \(X\) (in the application \(X = X_s\), a fibre over a
53	field \(\kappa(s)\)). Its category of sheaves of modules is
54	\(X.\Modules = \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\), with global-sections notation
55	\(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{M}) = \mathcal{M}(X)\) for the value of the underlying
56	presheaf on the top open. The structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) is a sheaf of
57	commutative rings, so its underlying presheaf factors through the category of
58	commutative rings; this is the hypothesis under which Mathlib's presheaf-level
59	monoidal structure applies.
60	
61	\begin{lemma}[Monoidal structure on presheaves of modules]
62	  \label{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
63	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}
64	  \mathlibok
65	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
66	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal}).}
67	  Let \(R : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
68	  commutative rings. Then the category \(\PshMod(R)\) of presheaves of modules
69	  over \(R\) carries a symmetric monoidal structure whose tensor product is
70	  computed objectwise: for presheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2\)
71	  and an object \(U\),
72	  \[
73	    (\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes \mathcal{M}_2)(U)
74	      \;=\; \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{R(U)} \mathcal{M}_2(U),
75	  \]
76	  with restriction maps the tensor products of the restriction maps. The unit is
77	  the presheaf \(R\) viewed as a module over itself, and the associator,
78	  unitors, and braiding are induced objectwise from those of
79	  \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\). In particular evaluation at a fixed object \(U\) is a
80	  \emph{strict} monoidal functor
81	  \(\PshMod(R) \to \mathrm{ModuleCat}(R(U))\).
82	\end{lemma}

217	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification inverts the whiskered localization unit]
218	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
219	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
220	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
221	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
222	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules,
223	  let \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\) be the unit of
224	  the sheafification adjunction
225	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}), viewed as a morphism of
226	  presheaves of modules, and write
227	  \[
228	    \eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} :
229	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
230	    \longrightarrow (\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
231	  \]
232	  for its right whiskering by \(\mathcal{Q}\) in the presheaf monoidal structure
233	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Then the image of
234	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) under the sheafification functor,
235	  \[
236	    (\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#} :
237	    (\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})^{\#}
238	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
239	    \bigl((\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\bigr)^{\#},
240	  \]
241	  is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules. This is the strong-monoidality
242	  comparison of the module sheafification functor on a whiskered unit.
243	\end{lemma}
244	
245	\begin{proof}
246	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
247	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
248	  Module sheafification is a localization functor: it inverts exactly the class
249	  \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying morphism of
250	  abelian-group presheaves is a local isomorphism --- that is, lies in the class
251	  \(J.W\) of weak equivalences attached to the Grothendieck topology \(J\) on
252	  \(X\) (the inverse image of \(J.W\) along the forgetful functor to
253	  abelian-group presheaves). By the localization criterion for a reflective
254	  adjunction, \((f)^{\#}\) is an isomorphism if and only if \(f \in W\), i.e.\
255	  if and only if the underlying abelian-presheaf morphism of \(f\) lies in
256	  \(J.W\). It therefore suffices to prove that the underlying abelian morphism
257	  of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) lies in \(J.W\).
258	
259	  \textbf{The relative tensor as a coequalizer.} The underlying abelian-group
260	  presheaf of the relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\)
261	  is the objectwise relative tensor
262	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\). For each
263	  \(U\) this module is the coequalizer of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action
264	  maps
265	  \[
266	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
267	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
268	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
269	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
270	  \]
271	  one acting on the left and one on the right factor. Colimits of abelian-group
272	  presheaves are computed objectwise, so \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\),
273	  as an abelian-group presheaf, is the coequalizer of the corresponding parallel
274	  pair
275	  \[
276	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
277	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
278	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
279	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
280	  \]
281	  of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaves. Under this presentation the underlying
282	  abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) is the map of
283	  coequalizers induced by whiskering the two rows with
284	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (-)\).
285	
286	  \textbf{Abelian sheafification preserves the coequalizer.} The abelian
287	  sheafification functor \(a\) (the associated-sheaf functor for \(J\) on
288	  abelian-group presheaves) is a left adjoint, hence preserves coequalizers; it
289	  therefore carries the parallel pair and its induced map to the analogous data
290	  of \(a\)-images. Now the localization unit \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}}\) lies in
291	  \(J.W\), and the class \(J.W\) is \emph{monoidal} for the
292	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor: whiskering a local isomorphism by
293	  \(\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\) again yields a local isomorphism. (This is the
294	  abelian monoidality of \(J.W\), itself proved through the fact that the
295	  internal hom into a sheaf is again a sheaf.) Consequently the two whiskered
296	  comparison maps
297	  \(a\bigl(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\bigr)\) and
298	  \(a\bigl(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}
299	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\bigr)\) relating the two rows are
300	  isomorphisms.
301	
302	  An isomorphism between two parallel-pair diagrams induces an isomorphism of
303	  their coequalizers; applied to the rows above, this shows that \(a\) sends the
304	  underlying abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) to an
305	  isomorphism. Hence that morphism lies in \(J.W\), so
306	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} \in W\), and
307	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#}\) is an isomorphism.
308	\end{proof}
309	
310	\begin{corollary}[Sheaf-level associator on tensor products]
311	  \label{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}
312	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjAssoc}
313	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
314	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
315	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
316	  For sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B},
317	  \mathcal{C}\) there is a canonical associativity isomorphism
318	  \[
319	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}} :
320	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{B})
321	      \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C}
322	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
323	    \mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
324	      (\mathcal{B} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C})
325	  \]
326	  of sheaves of modules (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}), natural in all three
327	  arguments.
328	\end{corollary}
329	
330	\begin{proof}
331	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
332	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
333	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
334	  Write \(a, b, c\) for the underlying presheaves of modules of
335	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) and \((-)^{\#}\) for sheafification
336	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}). By
337	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} the two iterated products unfold to
338	  \[
339	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
340	      = \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#},
341	    \qquad
342	    \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
343	      = \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#},
344	  \]
345	  each carrying an \emph{inner} sheafification that obstructs a direct appeal to
346	  the presheaf associator. The whiskered units remove these inner
347	  sheafifications. Applying \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
348	  with \(\mathcal{P} = a \otimes_p b\) and \(\mathcal{Q} = c\), the morphism
349	  \[
350	    (\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#} :
351	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
352	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
353	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
354	    = (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
355	  \]
356	  is an isomorphism; and, reading the same lemma through the braiding of the
357	  presheaf monoidal structure (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) so as
358	  to whisker the unit \(\eta_{b \otimes_p c}\) on the left of \(a\), the
359	  morphism
360	  \[
361	    (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#} :
362	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)\bigr)^{\#}
363	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
364	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#}
365	    = \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
366	  \]
367	  is an isomorphism. Between the two un-sheafified middle terms sits the
368	  sheafification of the presheaf associator
369	  \[
370	    \alpha^p_{a,b,c} :
371	    (a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c
372	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
373	    a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)
374	  \]
375	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), an isomorphism because
376	  sheafification is a functor and \(\alpha^p\) is one. The associator is the
377	  composite
378	  \[
379	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}}
380	    = (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#}
381	      \circ (\alpha^p_{a,b,c})^{\#}
382	      \circ \bigl((\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#}\bigr)^{-1},
383	  \]
384	  an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
385	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) follows from naturality of the unit,
386	  of the whiskerings, and of the presheaf associator.
387	\end{proof}

389	\begin{lemma}[Tensor-power comparison isomorphism]
390	  \label{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
391	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPowAdd}
392	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
393	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules"
394	  % (Tag 01CU, following `definition-powers') (read from
395	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4249-L4254).
396	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
397	  % "With this definition we have canonical isomorphisms
398	  %  $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
399	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \to
400	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m}$, and these isomorphisms
401	  %  satisfy a commutativity and an associativity constraint
402	  %  (formulation omitted)."
403	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (canonical
404	  power isomorphisms).}
405	  For a line bundle \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(m, m' \in \mathbb{N}\) there is a
406	  canonical isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
407	  \[
408	    \mu_{m,m'} :
409	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
410	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m')},
411	  \]
412	  natural in \(\mathcal{L}\), and these isomorphisms satisfy a commutativity
413	  constraint (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) agrees with \(\mu_{m',m}\) after the braiding) and
414	  an associativity constraint (the two ways of combining
415	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
416	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''}\) into \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\) agree).
417	\end{lemma}
418	
419	\begin{proof}
420	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj, cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc,
421	    lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
422	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
423	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
424	  We do \emph{not} build a full monoidal structure on the whole category
425	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) --- none is constructed here --- but only assemble
426	  the comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\) from those structural isomorphisms of
427	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} that the induction actually uses: the associator,
428	  the braiding, and the left unitor.
429	
430	  \textbf{The structural maps.} The braiding and the unitors descend to
431	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) directly: each is the sheafification of the
432	  corresponding isomorphism of the presheaf symmetric monoidal structure
433	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and sheafification, being a
434	  functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. The \emph{associator} is not
435	  the sheafification of a single presheaf isomorphism, because the iterated
436	  sheaf tensor products
437	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}\) and
438	  \(\mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})\) each carry an inner
439	  sheafification (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); it is supplied instead by
440	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, whose construction clears those inner
441	  sheafifications using the strong-monoidality comparison
442	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}. These are the only
443	  isomorphisms of sheaves of modules the construction invokes; right-exactness
444	  of the tensor product is never used.
445	
446	  \textbf{Construction of \(\mu_{m,m'}\).} Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For
447	  \(m = 0\),
448	  \[
449	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
450	      = \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
451	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
452	  \]
453	  is the left unitor of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}. For the inductive step the
454	  recursive definition
455	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}\)
456	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}) gives
457	  \[
458	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
459	    = (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \otimes
460	      \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
461	    \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
462	      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})
463	    \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{id} \otimes \beta\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
464	      (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes \mathcal{L}),
465	  \]
466	  using the associator \(\alpha\) (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and then the
467	  braiding \(\beta\) of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} whiskered into the left
468	  factor. The inverse associator \(\alpha^{-1}\)
469	  (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and the inductive comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\)
470	  whiskered by \(\mathcal{L}\) on the right then carry this to
471	  \[
472	    (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}) \otimes
473	      \mathcal{L}
474	    \xrightarrow{\ \mu_{m,m'} \rhd \mathcal{L}\ }
475	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m')} \otimes \mathcal{L}
476	    = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m + m') + 1)},
477	  \]
478	  the final equality being the recursive definition. The reindexing
479	  \((m + m') + 1 = (m + 1) + m'\) identifies the target with
480	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m+1) + m')}\), and the composite is
481	  \(\mu_{m+1,m'}\), an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
482	  \(\mathcal{L}\) holds because each constituent comparison is natural.
483	
484	  \textbf{Commutativity and associativity constraints.} The braiding and
485	  unitors are the sheafifications of the symmetric-monoidal coherence data of
486	  \(\PshMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and
487	  the associator is intertwined with the presheaf associator by the comparison
488	  isomorphisms of \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} through the
489	  construction of \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}. Both the commutativity square
490	  (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) versus \(\mu_{m',m}\) postcomposed with the braiding) and the
491	  associativity pentagon (the two bracketings of
492	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
493	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''} \to \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\)) are therefore
494	  images, under the sheafification functor together with these comparison
495	  isomorphisms, of the corresponding coherence diagrams of the presheaf
496	  symmetric monoidal structure, which commute by Mac\,Lane coherence. Since a
497	  functor preserves commuting diagrams and the comparison isomorphisms are
498	  natural, the constraints hold for the family \(\{\mu_{m,m'}\}\). We emphasise
499	  that this argument uses only the single strong-monoidality comparison
500	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} and the resulting associator
501	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, not a full monoidal structure on
502	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
503	\end{proof}
504	
505	\section{Lax-monoidal global sections}
506	\label{sec:sgr_lax_sections}
507	
508	The graded multiplication is supplied by the lax-monoidal structure of the
509	global-sections functor \(\Gamma(X, -) : \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
510	\mathrm{ModuleCat}(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))\). It is only \emph{lax} because
511	global sections do not commute with sheafification: a global section of a
512	sheafified tensor product is not in general a finite sum of elementary tensors of
513	global sections.
514	
515	\begin{definition}\leanok
516	[Section multiplication]
517	  \label{def:sectionMul}
518	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul}
519	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
520	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
521	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
522	  \emph{section multiplication} is the \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear map
523	  \[
524	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)}
525	      \Gamma(X, \mathcal{G})
526	    \;\longrightarrow\;
527	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
528	  \]
529	  defined as follows. A pair of global sections \((\sigma, \tau)\) determines,
530	  by the objectwise formula of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} at the
531	  top open, the elementary tensor \(\sigma \otimes \tau \in
532	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(X)\), a global section of
533	  the tensor product presheaf. Composing with the global-sections component of
534	  the sheafification unit
535	  \(\eta : \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
536	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^{\#} =
537	  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\)
538	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
539	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}) yields a global section
540	  \(\sigma \cdot \tau \in
541	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\). Bilinearity over
542	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is inherited from bilinearity of the elementary
543	  tensor and \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linearity of \(\eta\).
544	\end{definition}
545	
546	\begin{lemma}[Coherence of the section multiplication]
547	  \label{lem:sectionMul_coherent}
548	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_assoc_unit}
549	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib, lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
550	  The section multiplications (\cref{def:sectionMul}) are associative and unital
551	  in the following sense. For sheaves \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H}\)
552	  the two composites
553	  \[
554	    \Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{G}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{H})
555	    \longrightarrow
556	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H}\bigr)
557	    \cong
558	    \Gamma\bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes (\mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H})\bigr)
559	  \]
560	  agree after transport along the associator of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}; and
561	  the unit element \(1 \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
562	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) acts as a two-sided identity, i.e.\ the section
563	  multiplication by \(1\) is, after the unitor isomorphism
564	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{F} \cong \mathcal{F}\), the identity on
565	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\).
566	\end{lemma}
567	
568	\begin{proof}
569	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
570	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
571	  Both statements reduce to the corresponding identities at the level of the
572	  tensor product presheaf, where by \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
573	  evaluation at the top open is strictly monoidal, so the associator and unitors
574	  act on elementary tensors by the usual formulas
575	  \((\sigma \otimes \tau) \otimes \upsilon \mapsto
576	  \sigma \otimes (\tau \otimes \upsilon)\) and
577	  \(1 \otimes \sigma \mapsto \sigma\). The sheafification unit \(\eta\) is a
578	  natural transformation, so it commutes with the associator and unitor
579	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); applying \(\Gamma(X,-)\)
580	  to the naturality squares transports these presheaf-level identities to the
581	  section multiplications.
582	\end{proof}

584	\section{Graded-ring and graded-module assembly}
585	\label{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}
586	
587	The components and multiplications of the previous two sections are assembled
588	into the graded ring and graded module through Mathlib's
589	\emph{external-direct-sum} graded API: the relevant inputs are families of
590	abelian groups indexed by \(\mathbb{N}\) together with multiplication maps
591	landing in the index-sum component, not submodules of a pre-existing ring.
592	
593	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded commutative semiring]
594	  \label{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
595	  \lean{DirectSum.GCommSemiring}
596	  \mathlibok
597	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.DirectSum.Ring}).}
598	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
599	  monoids equipped with a graded multiplication
600	  \(A_i \times A_j \to A_{i+j}\) and a unit \(1 \in A_0\) satisfying graded
601	  associativity, two-sided unitality, distributivity, and graded commutativity
602	  (the data of a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\) on \(A\)). Then the external direct
603	  sum \(\bigoplus_i A_i\) is a commutative semiring, with multiplication the
604	  bilinear extension of the graded multiplication and identity the image of
605	  \(1 \in A_0\).
606	\end{lemma}
607	
608	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded module]
609	  \label{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}
610	  \lean{DirectSum.Gmodule}
611	  \mathlibok
612	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Module.GradedModule}).}
613	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) carry a graded semiring structure as
614	  in \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib} and let
615	  \(M : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
616	  monoids with a graded scalar action \(A_i \times M_j \to M_{i+j}\) satisfying
617	  the graded module axioms (the data of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\) of \(M\) over
618	  \(A\)). Then the external direct sum \(\bigoplus_j M_j\) is a module over the
619	  semiring \(\bigoplus_i A_i\), with scalar multiplication the bilinear
620	  extension of the graded action.
621	\end{lemma}
622	
623	\begin{lemma}[Graded semiring structure on the section components]
624	  \label{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
625	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
626	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
627	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib,
628	    lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
629	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
630	  % preceding `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
631	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4257-L4267).
632	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
633	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. We can define a $\mathbf{Z}$-graded
634	  %  ring structure on $\bigoplus \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ by mapping
635	  %  $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ and
636	  %  $t \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m})$ to the
637	  %  section corresponding to $s \otimes t$ in
638	  %  $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m})$.
639	  %  We omit the verification that this defines a commutative
640	  %  and associative ring with $1$."
641	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
642	  ring structure on \(\bigoplus \Gamma(X,\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})\)).}
643	  Let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on \(X_s\). The family of
644	  \(\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})\)-modules
645	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) carries a graded commutative
646	  semiring structure (a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\),
647	  \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}) whose degree-\((m,m')\)
648	  multiplication is the composite
649	  \[
650	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
651	      \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'})
652	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
653	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'}\bigr)
654	    \xrightarrow{\ \Gamma(\mu_{m,m'})\ }
655	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
656	  \]
657	  the section multiplication (\cref{def:sectionMul}) followed by the
658	  tensor-power comparison isomorphism (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), with unit
659	  \(1 \in \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s}) = \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes 0})\)
660	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}). Consequently
661	  \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
662	  commutative semiring; this is the graded-ring structure underlying
663	  \cref{def:sectionGradedRing}.

666	\begin{proof}
667	  \uses{lem:sheafTensorPow_add, lem:sectionMul_coherent,
668	    lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
669	  The graded multiplication maps are the composites displayed in the statement.
670	  Graded associativity is the associativity coherence of
671	  \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} combined with the associativity constraint of
672	  the comparison isomorphisms \(\mu\) (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}); graded
673	  commutativity is the unit/commutativity of the section multiplication combined
674	  with the commutativity constraint of \(\mu\); two-sided unitality is the unit
675	  coherence of \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} together with
676	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\). These are exactly the axioms of a
677	  \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\), so \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
678	  endows \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) with its
679	  commutative-semiring structure.

682	\begin{lemma}[Graded module structure on the twisted-section components]
683	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_gmodule}
684	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_gmodule}

1	\chapter{The tautological quotient and the universal property of $\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)$}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianQuot}
3	
4	The previous chapter constructed the Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
5	\(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing the affine charts \(U^I\)
6	(\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) along the transition isomorphisms
7	(\cref{def:gr_transition}) satisfying the cocycle condition
8	(\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}), and proved that the structure morphism
9	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated})
10	and proper (\cref{lem:gr_proper}). This chapter adds the next layer: the
11	\emph{tautological rank-\(d\) quotient}
12	\(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of the
13	trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf, and the \emph{universal property} that makes
14	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) the fine moduli space of rank-\(d\) locally free quotients of
15	\(\mathcal{O}^{r}\) -- equivalently, the representing scheme of the Quot functor
16	of the trivial sheaf.
17	
18	Throughout, \(r \ge d \ge 1\) are the fixed integers of the previous chapter, and
19	for a size-\(d\) subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) we write \(R^I\) for the
20	chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) of \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) and \(R^I_J\) for the
21	localisation along \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) defining the overlap
22	\(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\).
23	
24	\section{Infrastructure: locally free sheaves and module gluing}
25	\label{sec:grquot_infra}
26	
27	The constructions of this chapter -- the universal quotient sheaf
28	\(\mathcal{U}\), the tautological quotient \(u\), and the functor of points -- rest
29	on two pieces of foundational machinery for sheaves of modules that are not
30	available off the shelf and are therefore set up here. The first is the predicate
31	``locally free of rank \(d\)'', which records that a sheaf of modules is, on the
32	members of some open cover, isomorphic to the trivial bundle \(\mathcal{O}^{d}\);
33	it is the condition cut out in the definition of the Grassmannian functor. The
34	second is a gluing primitive that descends a sheaf of modules (and a morphism of
35	such sheaves) from per-chart data along an open cover, given a transition cocycle
36	compatible with the cover's own gluing; it is the vehicle that assembles the
37	per-chart free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}^{d}_{U^I}\) and the per-chart quotients
38	\(u^I\) into the global \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(u\).
39	
40	\begin{definition}
41	[Locally free of rank \(d\)]
42	  \label{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
43	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}
44	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{M}\) a sheaf of
45	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (an object of \(X.\mathtt{Modules}\)), and let
46	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). We say \(\mathcal{M}\) is \emph{locally free of rank
47	  \(d\)} if there exists an open cover \(\{U_i\}_{i}\) of \(X\) -- a family of
48	  opens with \(\bigcup_i U_i = X\) -- such that for each index \(i\) the
49	  restriction \(\mathcal{M}|_{U_i}\) of \(\mathcal{M}\) to \(U_i\) is isomorphic,
50	  as a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules, to the trivial rank-\(d\) bundle
51	  \[
52	    \mathcal{M}|_{U_i} \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\,d}
53	      \;=\; \bigoplus_{k \in \{1,\dots,d\}} \mathcal{O}_{U_i},
54	  \]
55	  the free sheaf on the index set of size \(d\). This is the analogue, for the
56	  rank-\(d\) free model, of the ``open cover together with a local isomorphism''
57	  shape used to express quasi-coherence and local finite presentation: it
58	  asserts the existence of a trivialising cover on which \(\mathcal{M}\) acquires
59	  a basis of \(d\) sections. A sheaf that is locally free of rank \(d\) is in
60	  particular quasi-coherent and locally of finite presentation, and its fibre at
61	  every point is a \(d\)-dimensional vector space. This is the condition imposed
62	  on the member sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) in a family of rank-\(d\) quotients
63	  (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}); the universal sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)
64	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}) satisfies it tautologically, with the
65	  charts \(\{U^I\}\) as trivialising cover.
66	\end{definition}
67	
68	\begin{definition}
69	[Gluing a sheaf of modules along an open cover]
70	  \label{def:scheme_modules_glue}
71	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue}
72	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
73	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (a \(\mathtt{Scheme.GlueData}\),
74	  \cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with index set \(J\), charts \(U_i := D.U(i)\),
75	  pairwise overlaps \(V_{ij} := D.V(i,j)\), open immersions
76	  \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow U_i\), and transition isomorphisms
77	  \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\) satisfying the glue datum's
78	  cocycle conditions; write \(X := D.\mathtt{glued}\) for the glued scheme and
79	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\) for the canonical open immersions of the
80	  charts. Suppose given:
81	  \begin{itemize}
82	    \item for each \(i \in J\), a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) on the chart
83	      \(U_i\) (an object of \(U_i.\mathtt{Modules}\));
84	    \item for each pair \((i,j)\), a \emph{transition isomorphism} of sheaves of
85	      modules on the overlap \(V_{ij}\),
86	      \[
87	        g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
88	          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
89	        (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j,
90	      \]
91	      comparing the two charts' sheaves after restriction (pullback) to the
92	      common overlap \(V_{ij}\);
93	  \end{itemize}
94	  subject to the \emph{module cocycle conditions} compatible with \(D\): the
95	  self-transition \(g_{ii}\) is the identity, and over each triple overlap the
96	  composite \(g_{jk} \circ g_{ij}\) agrees with \(g_{ik}\) once transported
97	  through the scheme-level triple transition of \(D\) (the multiplicative,
98	  \(\mathrm{GL}_d\)-valued cocycle condition for the family
99	  \((g_{ij})\)). Out of this data the construction produces:
100	  \begin{enumerate}
101	    \item a glued sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{M}\) on \(X = D.\mathtt{glued}\) (an
102	      object of \(X.\mathtt{Modules}\)); and
103	    \item for each \(i \in J\) a \emph{restriction isomorphism}
104	      \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\,\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\)
105	      identifying the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}\) along \(\iota_i\) with the
106	      \(i\)-th chart sheaf, the \(\rho_i\) being compatible with the transition
107	      isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps.
108	  \end{enumerate}
109	  The pair \((\mathcal{M}, (\rho_i)_i)\) is characterised up to unique
110	  isomorphism by the restriction property: \(\mathcal{M}\) is the unique sheaf of
111	  modules on \(X\) (up to canonical isomorphism) restricting to each
112	  \(\mathcal{M}_i\) compatibly with \((g_{ij})\). The same primitive descends
113	  morphisms: given two such glued data \((\mathcal{M}_i, g_{ij})\) and
114	  \((\mathcal{N}_i, h_{ij})\) with glued sheaves \(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}\), and
115	  a family of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-linear morphisms
116	  \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) that commute with the transition
117	  isomorphisms on every overlap, there is a unique morphism
118	  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) with
119	  \(\rho_i^{\mathcal{N}} \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi = \phi_i \circ \rho_i^{\mathcal{M}}\)
120	  for every \(i\) -- the descent of \(\mathrm{Hom}\) along the cover.
121	
122	  \emph{Construction.} A sheaf of modules on \(X\) is equivalent to a compatible
123	  family of sheaves on the members of an open cover of \(X\) together with
124	  identifications on the overlaps -- effective descent for the Zariski cover
125	  \(\{\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\}\). The charts \(U_i\), being the images of
126	  the glue datum's objects, form such a cover, with intersections
127	  \(U_i \cap U_j\) identified with the overlaps \(V_{ij}\) via the structural data
128	  of \(D\). Pulling the sections of an abstract \(\mathcal{M}\) back to each chart
129	  must reproduce \(\mathcal{M}_i\); the transition isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\),
130	  satisfying the cocycle conditions, are exactly the descent datum that makes the
131	  family \((\mathcal{M}_i)\) glue, so the descent produces \(\mathcal{M}\) with
132	  the asserted restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i\). The morphism statement is the
133	  descent of morphisms for the same cover: a morphism on \(X\) is a family of
134	  morphisms on the charts agreeing on overlaps, and the compatibility of the
135	  \(\phi_i\) with \((g_{ij}, h_{ij})\) is precisely the agreement condition,
136	  giving the unique glued \(\phi\). This specialises the general descent of sheaves
137	  of modules to the concrete open cover supplied by a \(\mathtt{Scheme.GlueData}\),
138	  and is the form in which the Grassmannian's tautological bundle and quotient are
139	  built.
140	\end{definition}
141	
142	\section{The tautological quotient on the charts}
143	\label{sec:grquot_chart}
144	
145	\begin{definition}
146	[Chart quotient \(u^I\)]
147	  \label{def:gr_chart_quotient}
148	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap}
149	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix}
150	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
151	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
152	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L898-L910).
153	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
154	  % "We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
155	  %  together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
156	  %  On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism
157	  %  $u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
158	  %  by the matrix $X^I$."
159	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
160	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). On the affine chart
161	  \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) define the
162	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}\)-linear homomorphism of trivial bundles
163	  \[
164	    u^I \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}
165	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}
166	  \]
167	  given by left multiplication by the universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\)
168	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}): on global sections it is the \(R^I\)-linear
169	  map \((R^I)^{r} \to (R^I)^{d}\), \(v \mapsto X^I v\). Since the \(I\)-th minor
170	  \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\) is the identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\), the composite of
171	  \(u^I\) with the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates
172	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) is the
173	  identity; hence \(u^I\) is a split surjection of locally free sheaves, with
174	  target the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\).
175	\end{definition}
176	
177	\begin{definition}
178	[The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)]
179	  \label{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}
180	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalQuotient}
181	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_transition,
182	    lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:scheme_modules_glue,
183	    def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
184	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
185	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
186	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L903-L910).
187	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
188	  % "Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
189	  %  for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
190	  %  $(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
191	  %  by putting
192	  %  $$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$"
193	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
194	  Over each overlap \(U^I_J = \Spec R^I_J\) define the bundle-transition matrix
195	  \[
196	    g_{I,J} \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} \;\in\; \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J),
197	  \]
198	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
199	  which is invertible because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\). The matrices
200	  \((g_{I,J})\) are compatible with the chart-gluing cocycle \((\theta_{I,J})\)
201	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}): over a triple overlap they
202	  satisfy \(g_{I,K} = g_{J,K}\, g_{I,J}\) and \(g_{I,I} = 1\), the multiplicative
203	  cocycle condition for a vector bundle. Gluing the free rank-\(d\) sheaves
204	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) along the isomorphisms \(g_{I,J}\) over the cover
205	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) therefore
206	  produces a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-module
207	  \(\mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). Its determinant line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\)
208	  has transition functions \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J\), the data underlying the
209	  Pl\"ucker embedding.
210	\end{definition}
211	
212	\begin{definition}
213	[The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}^{r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\)]
214	  \label{def:tautological_quotient}
215	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient}
216	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_chart_quotient,
217	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle,
218	    def:scheme_modules_glue}
219	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
220	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
221	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L908-L910).
222	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
223	  % "This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$,
224	  %  so we get a surjective homomorphism
225	  %  $u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$."
226	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
227	  The chart quotients \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
228	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) are compatible with the bundle gluing
229	  \((g_{I,J})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}): over
230	  each overlap \(U^I_J\) one has the identity of \(d \times r\) matrices
231	  \[
232	    g_{I,J}\, X^I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I \;=\; X^J \qquad \text{on } U^I_J,
233	  \]
234	  which is precisely the defining formula of the chart transition
235	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}).
236	  Hence the local maps \(u^I\), transported through the trivialisations of
237	  \(\mathcal{U}\), agree on overlaps and glue to a single surjective
238	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-linear homomorphism
239	  \[
240	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
241	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},
242	  \]
243	  the \emph{tautological} (or \emph{universal}) rank-\(d\) quotient of the trivial
244	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
245	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
246	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
247	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
248	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.
249	\end{definition}

251	\section{The functor of points}
252	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
253	
254	\begin{definition}
255	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
256	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
257	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
258	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:quot_functor}
259	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
260	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
261	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
262	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
263	  % "represents the
264	  %  contravariant functor
265	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
266	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
267	  %  set of all equivalence classes
268	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
269	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
270	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
271	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
272	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
273	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
274	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
275	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
276	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
277	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
278	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
279	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
280	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
281	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
282	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
283	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
284	  \[
285	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
286	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
287	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
288	  \]
289	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
290	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
291	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
292	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
293	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
294	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
295	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
296	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
297	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
298	\end{definition}
299	
300	\section{The universal property}
301	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
302	
303	\begin{theorem}
304	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
305	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
306	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
307	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
308	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
309	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
310	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
311	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
312	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
313	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
314	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
315	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
316	  %  contravariant functor
317	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
318	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
319	  %  set of all equivalence classes
320	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
321	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
322	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
323	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
324	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
325	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
326	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
327	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
328	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
329	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
330	  \[
331	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
332	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
333	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
334	    \qquad
335	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
336	      \;\longmapsto\;
337	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
338	  \]
339	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
340	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
341	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
342	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
343	  universal family.
344	\end{theorem}
345	
346	\begin{proof}
347	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
348	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
349	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
350	    def:grassmannian_functor}
351	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
352	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
353	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
354	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
355	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
356	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
357	
358	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
359	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
360	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
361	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
362	  the composite
363	  \[
364	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
365	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
366	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
367	  \]
368	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
369	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
370	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
371	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
372	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
373	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
374	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
375	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
376	
377	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
378	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
379	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
380	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
381	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
382	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
383	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
384	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
385	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
386	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
387	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
388	
389	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
390	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
391	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
392	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
393	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
394	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
395	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
396	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\), so by the cocycle condition
397	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) the \(\varphi_I\) glue to a single morphism
398	  \(\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). By
399	  construction \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F},
400	  q \rangle\), since this holds on each \(T_I\) and the equivalence class is
401	  determined by \(\ker(q)\).
402	
403	  \emph{Uniqueness.} If \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) also satisfies
404	  \(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), then
405	  on \(\psi^{-1}(U^I)\) the pulled-back tautological quotient \(\psi^{*}u^I\)
406	  equals \(q\) in the \(I\)-trivialisation, which forces \(\psi^{*}X^I = M^I\) and
407	  hence \(\psi = \varphi_I\) there; as the \(U^I\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\),
408	  \(\psi = \varphi\). Thus \(\varphi\) is the unique morphism inducing
409	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), and \(\varphi \mapsto \langle
410	  \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u \rangle\) is bijective.
411	
412	  Therefore \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is a fine
413	  moduli space: it represents \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\), so
414	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
415	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
416	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	
6	\section{Setup and motivation}
7	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
8	
9	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
10	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
11	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
12	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
13	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
14	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
15	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
16	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
17	single non-zero element of the base ring.
18	
19	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
20	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
21	\(\F\).
22	
23	\section{Generic flatness}
24	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
25	
26	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
27	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
28	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
29	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
30	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
31	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
32	non-zero element of the base ring.
33	
34	\begin{theorem}
35	
36	
37	
38	\leanok
39	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
40	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
41	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
42	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
43	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
44	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
45	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
48	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
49	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
50	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
51	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
52	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
53	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
54	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
55	
56	\end{theorem}
57	
58	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
59	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
60	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
61	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
62	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
63	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
64	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
65	% there exists a finite filtration
66	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
67	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
68	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
69	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
70	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
71	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
72	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
73	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
74	\begin{proof}
75	
76	  \textbf{Primary route — the finite \(A\)-module case.}
77	  Suppose first that \(M\) is module-finite over \(A\) itself. Since \(A\) is noetherian,
78	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). The generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\)
79	  (where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\)) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free
80	  over \(K\); by \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} this freeness descends to a single basic open:
81	  there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\). This closes the
82	  theorem whenever \(M\) is finite over \(A\).
83	
84	  \textbf{Surviving residue — reducing the finite-type \(B\)-algebra case to the
85	  finite \(A\)-module case.} The genuine content not supplied by Mathlib is the
86	  reduction of the general hypothesis (\(M\) finite over a \emph{finite-type}
87	  \(A\)-algebra \(B\)) to the finite \(A\)-module situation handled above. Concretely,
88	  the missing piece is the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness: a finite module
89	  over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) becomes free over \(A_f\) after inverting a single
90	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). The classical \S4 dévissage below is the fallback route
91	  for exactly this residue.
92	
93	  \emph{Fallback (Nitsure \S4 induction).}
94	  The \S4 dévissage is assembled from the sub-lemmas of
95	  \cref{sec:gf_devissage_chain}. If \(M_K = 0\), the torsion base case
96	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} gives \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f = 0\) free. Otherwise the
97	  prime-filtration induction principle \cref{lem:noeth_prime_filtration} over the
98	  noetherian ring \(B\), combined with the splicing fact
99	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}, reduces to \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) and hence to
100	  \(B\) a domain, \(M = B\); Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators
101	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators} makes \(B_g\) finite over a polynomial
102	  ring \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the polynomial-ring core
103	  \cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core} — bottoming out at the finite-\(A\)-module leaf
104	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} of the primary route — produces the required
105	  \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\).
106	\end{proof}

108	\subsection{Dévissage chain for the finite-type residue}
109	\label{sec:gf_devissage_chain}

111	The following sub-lemmas decompose the surviving residue of
112	\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} — the case where \(M\) is finite over the
113	finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\) but not module-finite over \(A\) — into the
114	classical \cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 induction. They are ordered so that each block
115	depends only on earlier blocks or Mathlib lemmas. Throughout,
116	\(A\) is a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), \(B\) a
117	finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module with the compatible
118	\(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\).

120	\begin{lemma}\leanok

122	  \label{lem:gf_finite_module}

124	  \uses{lem:fp_free_descent}

129	\begin{proof}
130	
131	  This is the \(d = 0\) leaf of the induction. A finite module over a noetherian ring is
132	  finitely presented, so
133	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). Over the generic point
134	  \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A) = S^{-1}A\) (\(S = A \setminus \{0\}\)) the localisation
135	  \(M_K = S^{-1}M\) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free over
136	  \(K\). The finite-presentation free-descent lemma \cref{lem:fp_free_descent}
137	  then descends this freeness to a single basic open: there is \(r \in S\), i.e.\
138	  \(r \in A\) with \(r \ne 0\), such that \(M_r\) is free over \(A_r\). Take
139	  \(f := r\).

226	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}

234	
235	  Because \(f = f' f''\), the localisation away from \(f\) factors through the
236	  localisation away from \(f'\): the ring \(A_f\) is the localisation of \(A_{f'}\)

238	  \(N_{f'}\) at the image of \(f''\). A localisation of a free module is free over

240	  \(N_f\) over \(A_f\). Hence \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Stated as a standalone

257	  of free \(A_f\)-modules is free, hence \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
258	\end{proof}
